1013	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1014	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1015	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1016	  % NOTE (shared engine): the single-variable Nagata change-of-variables together with
1017	  % the denominator-clearing after inverting one element of A is the SAME engine the
1018	  % Noether-normalisation step L4 (lem:gf_noether_clear_denominators, via its sub-lemma
1019	  % lem:gf_clear_one_denominator) requires. It should be built once in Lean and reused by
1020	  % both: a general "make a nonzero F monic in the last variable and clear the leading
1021	  % coefficient into one Localization.Away g" helper discharges this lemma and feeds L4.
1022	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1023	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1766-L1768).
1024	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1025	  % "Therefore, the dimension of the support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$
1026	  %  as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less than $n$. Hence by
1027	  %  induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),"
1028	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (the scaffold creates the decl pinned above with this signature).
1029	  %   Write A_g := Localization.Away g and T_g := LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T.
1030	  %     (A : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
1031	  %       (d : ℕ) (hd : 0 < d) (T : Type*) [AddCommGroup T]
1032	  %       [Module (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]

1034	  %       [Module A T] [IsScalarTower A (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T]
1035	  %       (htors : Module.IsTorsion (MvPolynomial (Fin d) A) T) :
1036	  %       ∃ (g : A) (_ : g ≠ 0) (m' : ℕ) (_ : m' < d)
1037	  %         (_ : Module (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1038	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T))
1039	  %         -- NOTE: the redundant canonical `Module A_g T_g` existential was
1040	  %         -- dropped from the landed decl (it is always `inferInstance`; bundling it created an
1041	  %         -- opaque fvar breaking OreLocalization instance matching at the L5 use-site).
1042	  %         (_ : IsScalarTower (Localization.Away g)
1043	  %                 (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1044	  %                 (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)),
1045	  %         Module.Finite (MvPolynomial (Fin m') (Localization.Away g))
1046	  %           (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers g) T)

1048	  %   accepts any m' < d.
1049	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (support-dimension drop).}
1050	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(d \ge 1\), and \(T\) a finite \emph{torsion} module
1051	  over \(P_d := A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) (with its induced \(A\)-module structure). Then there
1052	  exist a non-zero \(g \in A\) and an integer \(m' < d\) such that, after inverting \(g\),
1053	  the localised module \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1054	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in \(m'\) variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\);
1055	  that is, \(T_g\) carries compatible \(A_g\)- and \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module
1056	  structures (via the scalar tower \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)) and is finite over
1057	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). One may always take \(m' = d - 1\).
1058	\end{lemma}
1059	
1060	\begin{proof}
1061	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1766-L1768).
1062	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (support-dimension drop).}
1063	  \uses{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator, lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar,
1064	        lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic, lem:gf_pullback_module_transport,
1065	        lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv, lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1066	  Write \(d = (d-1) + 1\) and \(P_d = A[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) (the distinguished variable
1067	  \(X_0\) is the one eliminated in the single-variable step; cf.\ \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar}).
1068	
1069	  \emph{Annihilator.} By \cref{lem:gf_torsion_annihilator} there is a non-zero
1070	  \(F \in \operatorname{Ann}_{P_d}(T)\), and \(T\) is a finite module over \(P_d/(F)\).
1071	
1072	  \emph{Nagata normalisation.} Applying \cref{lem:gf_nagata_monic_lastVar} to \(F \ne 0\)
1073	  yields a triangular \(A\)-algebra automorphism \(e\) of \(P_d\) and a non-zero
1074	  \(g \in A\) such that the image of \(e(F)\) in \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\) has a unit
1075	  leading coefficient in \(X_0\). The automorphism \(e\) induces a ring isomorphism
1076	  \(P_d/(F) \cong P_d/(e\,F)\), so \(T\) is also finite over \(P_d/(e\,F)\). Localising at
1077	  the powers of \(g\) (localisation commutes with the quotient), \(T_g\) is finite over
1078	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(e\,F) = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\).
1079	
1080	  \emph{Elimination.} Apply the shared engine
1081	  \cref{lem:gf_mvPolynomial_quotient_finite_monic} with \(R = A_g\) and \(p\) the image of
1082	  \(e(F)\) (whose leading coefficient in \(X_0\) is a unit): the quotient
1083	  \(A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]/(e\,F)\) is module-finite over the coefficient subring
1084	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), a polynomial ring in \(m' := d - 1\) variables over
1085	  \(A_g\).
1086	
1087	  \emph{Localisation transport.} The elimination step records module-finiteness of the
1088	  \emph{quotient ring} over its coefficient subring, but the conclusion of the lemma is
1089	  about a localisation of the torsion module \(T\). Making the two agree is the
1090	  mathematical content of this step, which proceeds as follows.
1091	
1092	  Two distinct localisations of \(T\) are in play and must be identified. The natural
1093	  setting for the elimination is the localisation \(T'_g := T[\,\mathcal{C}^{-1}]\) of \(T\)
1094	  at the multiplicative set \(\mathcal{C} = \{\,c \cdot g^{\,k} : k \ge 0\,\}\) of constant
1095	  polynomials \(g^k\) inside \(P_d\); this localisation carries, for free, the full
1096	  module structure over the localised polynomial ring
1097	  \(P_d[g^{-1}] = A_g[X_0, \dots, X_{d-1}]\), its finiteness, and the quotient structure by
1098	  \((e\,F)\). The \emph{goal}, however, is phrased with \(T_g := T[g^{-1}]\), the
1099	  localisation of \(T\) at the powers of the single element \(g \in A\). One first proves
1100	  \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R := A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) and then transports that
1101	  finiteness to \(T_g\).
1102	
1103	  The change of variables \(e\) is transported to the localised polynomial ring as a
1104	  \emph{ring isomorphism} \(\bar e : P_d[g^{-1}] \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]\), obtained
1105	  by localising \(e\) at \(\mathcal{C}\) (which \(e\) preserves, since \(e\) fixes the
1106	  constants \(c\,X^0\)); it carries the localised annihilator \(F_g\) to the
1107	  Nagata-normalised generator \(G\) and fixes the constants coming from the base. Passing
1108	  to quotients, \(\bar e\) descends to a ring isomorphism
1109	  \(\psi : P_d[g^{-1}]/(F_g) \xrightarrow{\sim} P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) of the two quotient rings
1110	  (using that the image of the ideal \((F_g)\) under \(\bar e\) is \((G)\)). Both quotient
1111	  rings act on \(T'_g\), and module-finiteness over \(R\) of the elimination-side quotient
1112	  \(P_d[g^{-1}]/(G)\) is transported across \(\psi\) — a ring isomorphism of the
1113	  \emph{acting} ring compatible with the fixed \(R\)-algebra structure — by
1114	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}, yielding \(T'_g\) module-finite over \(R\).
1115	
1116	  Finally the two localisations are identified. Restricting scalars of the canonical
1117	  \(P_d\)-localisation map of \(T\) along \(A \to P_d\) exhibits \(T'_g\) as a localisation
1118	  of \(T\) \emph{at the powers of \(g\) over \(A\)} — this descent of the
1119	  localised-module property along the scalar tower is
1120	  \cref{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars} — so the universal property produces a
1121	  linear isomorphism \(T_g \cong T'_g\) identifying the two.
1122	
1123	  \emph{Action-diamond subtlety.} The conclusion of the lemma requires an
1124	  \(A_g\)-module structure on \(T_g\) sitting in a scalar tower
1125	  \(A_g \to A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}] \to T_g\). One cannot simply install an anonymous
1126	  \(A_g\)-action by fiat: \(T_g = T[g^{-1}]\) already carries its \emph{canonical}
1127	  localisation action of \(A_g\), and this canonical action is the one the scalar-tower
1128	  requirement sees. Hence the identification \(T_g \cong T'_g\) must be promoted to an
1129	  \(A_g\)-linear isomorphism — which it is, because the transported action agrees with the
1130	  canonical localisation action on the \(A_g\)-constants: the constant
1131	  \(\mathcal{C}\)-action of \(A_g\) on \(T'_g\) factors through the original \(A\)-action on
1132	  \(T\) exactly as the canonical \(g\)-localisation action does. With the isomorphism made
1133	  \(A_g\)-linear, the \(R\)-module structure and finiteness pull back from \(T'_g\) to
1134	  \(T_g\) by \cref{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}, and the scalar tower
1135	  \(A_g \to R \to T_g\) follows.
1136	
1137	  \emph{Transitivity.} Composing the two module-finite extensions, \(T_g\) is finite over
1138	  \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\). Since \(m' = d - 1 < d\) (using \(d \ge 1\)), this is the
1139	  required reindexing; no Krull-dimension theory is needed, the number of variables
1140	  dropping by exactly one through the single-variable elimination.
1141	\end{proof}

1143	\subsubsection{Module-structure transport helpers (reindex)}
1144	\label{sec:gf_reindex_transport_helpers}
1145	
1146	The localisation-transport step of \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} moves a module structure
1147	and its finiteness across (i) an additive equivalence of the underlying groups, (ii) a ring
1148	isomorphism of the acting ring, and (iii) a change of localising monoid along a scalar
1149	tower. The following helper lemmas package those three transport principles. They are general
1150	module-theory facts; no external source is cited.
1151	
1152	\begin{lemma}[Module transport along an additive equivalence]
1153	  \label{lem:gf_pullback_module_transport}
1154	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1155	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_pullbackModuleAddEquiv}
1156	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.pullback_isScalarTower}
1157	  Let \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} N\) be an additive equivalence and let \(M\) carry an
1158	  \(R\)-module structure. Pulling the action back along \(e\) by
1159	  \(r \cdot y := e\big(r \cdot e^{-1}(y)\big)\) makes \(N\) an \(R\)-module for which \(e\)
1160	  is \(R\)-linear. Under this transported structure: (a) if \(M\) is module-finite over
1161	  \(R\) then so is \(N\); and (b) if \(M\) additionally carries a compatible action of a
1162	  base ring \(A_g\) with scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to M\), then the pulled-back actions on
1163	  \(N\) again form a scalar tower \(A_g \to R \to N\).
1164	\end{lemma}
1165	\begin{proof}
1166	  The module axioms for \(y \mapsto e(r \cdot e^{-1}(y))\) follow termwise from those on
1167	  \(M\) together with the fact that \(e\) and \(e^{-1}\) are additive and mutually inverse;
1168	  by construction \(e\) is then an \(R\)-linear isomorphism. Statement (a) follows by
1169	  transporting module-finiteness across the \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\).
1170	  Statement (b) checks the scalar-tower identity
1171	  \((a\,r)\cdot y = a\cdot(r\cdot y)\) on \(N\) by transporting it across \(e\) and applying
1172	  associativity of the scalar action on \(M\).
1173	\end{proof}
1174	
1175	\begin{lemma}[Finite transport along a ring isomorphism of the acting ring]
1176	  \label{lem:gf_finite_of_quotient_ringequiv}
1177	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.finite_of_quotientRingEquiv}
1178	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring and let \(\psi : B_1 \xrightarrow{\sim} B_2\) be a ring
1179	  isomorphism between two \(R\)-algebras that is compatible with the structure maps, i.e.\
1180	  \(\psi \circ (\text{algebra map } R \to B_1) = (\text{algebra map } R \to B_2)\). If
1181	  \(B_2\) is module-finite over \(R\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B_1\) (with the
1182	  scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\)), then \(M\) is module-finite over \(R\).
1183	\end{lemma}
1184	\begin{proof}
1185	  Compatibility of \(\psi^{-1}\) with the structure maps follows from that of \(\psi\), so
1186	  \(\psi^{-1}\) is an \(R\)-algebra isomorphism; transporting \(R\)-finiteness of \(B_2\)
1187	  across it makes \(B_1\) module-finite over \(R\). Transitivity of module-finiteness through
1188	  the scalar tower \(R \to B_1 \to M\) then gives \(M\) module-finite over \(R\).
1189	\end{proof}
1190	
1191	\begin{lemma}[Descent of a localised-module structure along a scalar tower]
1192	  \label{lem:gf_islocalizedmodule_restrictscalars}
1193	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.isLocalizedModule_restrictScalars}
1194	  Let \(A_g \to R\) be a ring map with compatible \(A_g\)- and \(R\)-module structures on
1195	  \(M\) and \(M'\) (scalar towers \(A_g \to R \to M\) and \(A_g \to R \to M'\)), and let
1196	  \(S \subseteq A_g\) be a multiplicative subset. If an \(R\)-linear map
1197	  \(f : M \to M'\) realises \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at the image submonoid
1198	  \(S \cdot 1 \subseteq R\) over \(R\), then its restriction of scalars to \(A_g\) realises
1199	  \(M'\) as the localisation of \(M\) at \(S\) over \(A_g\).
1200	\end{lemma}
1201	\begin{proof}
1202	  One verifies the universal property of a localised module for the restricted map. Each
1203	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(M'\): its action coincides, via the scalar tower, with
1204	  that of its image in \(R\), which is a unit because \(f\) is a localisation at the image
1205	  submonoid. Surjectivity onto \(M'\) by fractions \(x/s\) with \(s \in S\) and the
1206	  equality criterion likewise descend from the corresponding image-submonoid statements,
1207	  rewriting each \(R\)-scalar by an \(A_g\)-scalar through the scalar tower. Assembling
1208	  these conditions gives the claim.
1209	\end{proof}
1210	
1211	\begin{lemma}[Localisation identification across an associated denominator]
1212	  \label{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated}
1213	  \lean{IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated}
1214	  \mathlibok
1215	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1216	  Let \(R \to S \to T\) be a tower of commutative rings with compatible structure maps, let
1217	  \(x, z \in R\) and \(y \in S\), and suppose \(S\) is the localisation of \(R\) away from
1218	  \(x\) and \(T\) is the localisation of \(S\) away from \(y\). If the image of \(z\) in \(S\)
1219	  is associated to \(y\) (the two differ by a unit), then \(T\) is the localisation of \(R\)
1220	  away from the single product \(x z\); that is, \(T \cong R[(x z)^{-1}]\).
1221	\end{lemma}
1222	
1223	\begin{lemma}[Freeness transports across a semilinear ring isomorphism]
1224	  \label{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1225	  \lean{Module.Free.of_ringEquiv}
1226	  \mathlibok
1227	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1228	  Let \(R, R'\) be rings, \(M\) an \(R\)-module and \(M'\) an \(R'\)-module, and let
1229	  \(e : M \xrightarrow{\sim} M'\) be an additive isomorphism that is semilinear over a ring
1230	  isomorphism \(\sigma : R \xrightarrow{\sim} R'\), i.e.\ \(e(r \cdot m) = \sigma(r) \cdot e(m)\).
1231	  If \(M\) is free over \(R\), then \(M'\) is free over \(R'\).
1232	\end{lemma}

1234	\begin{lemma}[L5 step-4 helper — descent of freeness across a tower of \(\mathrm{Away}\) localisations]
1235	  \label{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1236	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.free_localizationAway_of_away_tower}
1237	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1238	  % LEAN DEPS: the localisation-transport primitives the Lean proof consumes are
1239	  %   IsLocalization.surj                          (clear the denominator of h over A_g)
1240	  %   IsLocalization.map_units                     (the cleared denominator s ∈ powers g is a unit)
1241	  %   IsLocalization.Away.mul_of_associated        (key ring identification: Away(g·a) on A_h)
1242	  %   IsLocalization.algEquiv                      (ring iso Away(g·a) ≅ A_h)
1243	  %   Module.Free.of_ringEquiv                     (transport freeness across that ring iso)
1244	  %   IsLocalizedModule.linearEquiv                (the σ-semilinear module equivalence)
1245	  %   LinearEquiv.extendScalarsOfIsLocalization    (upgrade the R-linear equiv to Away(g·a)-linear)
1246	  % The one primitive NOT packaged by Mathlib — that the composite localisation map
1247	  %   ψ := mkLinearMap(powers h) ∘ mkLinearMap(powers g) : T → (T_g)_h
1248	  % satisfies IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ — is proved directly from the three
1249	  % IsLocalizedModule axioms (units act invertibly, surjectivity up to denominators, the
1250	  % equality criterion); there is no packaged localisation-of-localisation lemma to invoke.
1251	  % NOTE: the closed Lean proof DID find a packaged route — the composite-localisation
1252	  % `IsLocalizedModule (powers (g·a)) ψ` step goes through `IsBaseChange.comp` via
1253	  % `isLocalizedModule_iff_isBaseChange`, so the "no packaged lemma" remark above is superseded.
1254	  % WITNESS: the conclusion holds at the SINGLE product f := g·a (not at a square f²).
1255	  Let \(A\) be a domain and \(T\) an \(A\)-module. Let \(g \in A\), \(g \ne 0\), and let
1256	  \(h \in A_g := \mathrm{Away}(g)\) be non-zero. Write
1257	  \((T_g)_h\) for the iterated localisation of \(T\) — first away from \(g\), then away from
1258	  \(h\) — and \((A_g)_h := \mathrm{Away}(h)\) for the corresponding iterated localisation of the
1259	  base ring. If \((T_g)_h\) is free over \((A_g)_h\), then there exists \(f \in A\),
1260	  \(f \ne 0\), such that the single localisation \(T_f\) is free over \(A_f := \mathrm{Away}(f)\).
1261	  Concretely the witness is the single product \(f := g\,a\) for an element \(a \in A\) whose
1262	  image in \(A_g\) is associated to \(h\).
1263	\end{lemma}
1264	
1265	\begin{proof}
1266	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated, lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}
1267	  \emph{Clear the denominator of \(h\).} As \(h\) lies in \(A_g = \mathrm{Away}(g)\),
1268	  surjectivity of the localisation map \(A \to A_g\) onto fractions yields \(a \in A\) and a
1269	  power \(s\) of \(g\) with \(h \cdot \bar s = \bar a\) in \(A_g\), where \(\bar{\,\cdot\,}\)
1270	  denotes the image in \(A_g\). The image \(\bar s\) is a unit (a power of \(g\) is invertible
1271	  in \(A_g\)), so \(\bar a = h\,\bar s\) is associated to \(h\); in particular \(\bar a \ne 0\)
1272	  (because \(h \ne 0\)), hence \(a \ne 0\). Since \(A\) is a domain and \(g, a \ne 0\), the
1273	  product \(f := g\,a\) is non-zero.
1274	
1275	  \emph{Identify the base rings.} The associatedness of \(\bar a\) and \(h\), together with the
1276	  tower \(A \to A_g \to A_h\), identifies the iterated localisation
1277	  \((A_g)_h = \mathrm{Away}(h)\) with the single localisation of \(A\) away from the product
1278	  \(g\,a\): by \cref{lem:isLocalization_away_mul_of_associated} (with \(x = g\), \(z = a\),
1279	  \(y = h\)) the ring \(A_h\) is a localisation of \(A\) away from \(g\,a\), and uniqueness of
1280	  localisation furnishes a ring isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \xrightarrow{\sim} A_h\).
1281	
1282	  \emph{Identify the modules.} The composite localisation map
1283	  \[
1284	    \psi : T \longrightarrow (T_g)_h, \qquad
1285	    \psi := \mathrm{mk}_{\langle h\rangle} \circ \mathrm{mk}_{\langle g\rangle},
1286	  \]
1287	  first into \(T_g\) and then into \((T_g)_h\), realises \((T_g)_h\) as the localisation of
1288	  \(T\) at the powers of \(g\,a\): every \(g^n a^m\) acts invertibly on \((T_g)_h\), \(\psi\) is
1289	  surjective up to such denominators, and its kernel is the expected one. This
1290	  localisation-of-localisation identity is verified directly from the defining properties of a
1291	  localised module; no packaged lemma supplies it.
1292	
1293	  \emph{Transport freeness.} By hypothesis \((T_g)_h\) is free over \(A_h\). The ring
1294	  isomorphism \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a) \cong A_h\) and the induced semilinear module equivalence
1295	  \(T_{g\,a} \simeq (T_g)_h\) — assembled from the localised-module identification of the
1296	  previous step and promoted to an \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\)-linear equivalence — carry this
1297	  freeness back by \cref{lem:module_free_of_ringEquiv}: \(T_{g\,a}\) is free over
1298	  \(\mathrm{Away}(g\,a)\). Taking \(f := g\,a\) gives \(T_f\) free over \(A_f\), as required.
1299	\end{proof}

1301	\begin{lemma}[L5 — polynomial-ring core of generic freeness]
1302	  \label{lem:gf_polynomial_core}
1303	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_polynomial}
1304	  % NOTE (signature): the Lean decl uses a SHARED universe
1305	  %   `(A N : Type u)` (not `(A : Type*) (N : Type*)`). This is load-bearing, not a
1306	  %   convenience: the base-domain-generalizing strong induction applies its IH at
1307	  %   `A_g = Localization.Away g`, and `LocalizedModule S M : Type (max u_R u_M)`, so the
1308	  %   reindexed module escapes `N`'s universe unless `A` and `N` share one. The geometric
1309	  %   target lives entirely in `Type u` (`Scheme.{u}`), so this is the natural setting.
1310	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1311	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1312	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1313	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4 ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'', proof, inductive step
1314	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1760-L1772).
1315	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:
1316	  % "Let $m$ be the generic rank of the finite module $B_g$ over the domain
1317	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Then we have a short exact sequence of
1318	  %  $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$-modules of the form
1319	  %  $0\to A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]^{\oplus m} \to B_g \to T \to 0$ where $T$ is a finite
1320	  %  torsion module over $A_g[b_1,\ldots, b_n]$. Therefore, the dimension of the
1321	  %  support of $K\otimes_{A_g}T$ as a $K\otimes_{A_g}(B_g)$-module is strictly less
1322	  %  than $n$. Hence by induction on $n$ (applied to the data $A_g$, $B_g$, $T$),
1323	  %  there exists some $h\ne 0$ in $A$ with $T_h$ free over $A_{gh}$. Taking $f=gh$,
1324	  %  the lemma follows from the above short exact sequence."
1325	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, ``Lemma on Generic Flatness'' (polynomial-ring
1326	  core of the induction).}
1327	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(d \ge 0\). Let \(N\) be a finite module over
1328	  the polynomial ring \(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\), regarded as an \(A\)-module. Then there
1329	  exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1330	
1331	\end{lemma}
1332	
1333	\begin{proof}
1334	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module, lem:gf_splice_shortExact, lem:gf_torsion_base,
1335	        lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport, lem:gf_generic_rank_ses,
1336	        lem:gf_torsion_reindex, lem:gf_away_tower_descent}
1337	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (L1760-L1772).
1338	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4 (polynomial-ring core).}
1339	  % LEAN PROOF STRUCTURE: the induction must generalise the base domain A
1340	  % TOGETHER WITH N. Formally, prove
1341	  %   ∀ d, ∀ (A) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A] (N) [..module data..],
1342	  %        ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧ Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
1343	  %                              (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N)
1344	  % by Nat.strong_induction_on d, with A (and its CommRing/IsDomain/IsNoetherianRing
1345	  % instances) AND all of N's d-dependent module instances REVERTED INTO THE MOTIVE.
1346	  % This is load-bearing, and is the real cause of the prior stall: the reindex
1347	  % lem:gf_torsion_reindex changes the base ring from A to A_g = Localization.Away g, so
1348	  % the inductive hypothesis must be applied at base A_g, NOT A. An IH that fixes A would
1349	  % not typecheck on the reindexed module T_g (which is finite over
1350	  % MvPolynomial (Fin m') A_g). A_g remains a noetherian domain (a localisation of a
1351	  % noetherian domain), so the motive's hypotheses hold at A_g. Because `A` is already an
1352	  % explicit argument of the pinned signature, this is a reversion of A into the motive,
1353	  % NOT a change to the public signature. See analogies/gf-generic-rank-ses.md.
1354	  We argue by strong induction on \(d\), \emph{generalising the base domain \(A\)} (along
1355	  with the module \(N\)): the inductive hypothesis is available for every \(d' < d\),
1356	  every noetherian domain in place of \(A\), and every finite module over the
1357	  corresponding polynomial ring.
1358	
1359	  \emph{Base case \(d = 0\).} The ring \(P_0 = A[X_1, \dots, X_0]\) is \(A\) itself, so
1360	  \(N\) is a finite \(A\)-module and the claim is exactly the finite-module leaf
1361	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module}.
1362	
1363	  \emph{Inductive step \(d \ge 1\).} If \(N\) is already a torsion \(P_d\)-module over
1364	  \(A\) — equivalently \(N_K = 0\) for \(K = \mathrm{Frac}(A)\) — then the torsion base
1365	  case \cref{lem:gf_torsion_base} produces \(f \ne 0\) with \(N_f = 0\), which is free
1366	  over \(A_f\). Otherwise we proceed in five steps.
1367	  \begin{enumerate}
1368	    \item \textbf{Generic-rank SES.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_generic_rank_ses} to \(N\) over
1369	      \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\): there are \(m \in \mathbb{N}\) and an injective
1370	      \(P_d\)-linear map \(\varphi : P_d^{\oplus m} \to N\) with torsion cokernel
1371	      \(T := N / \operatorname{im}\varphi\), i.e.\ a short exact sequence of
1372	      \(P_d\)-modules
1373	      \[
1374	        0 \to P_d^{\oplus m} \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } N \to T \to 0,
1375	        \qquad T \text{ torsion}.
1376	      \]
1377	      No element of \(A\) is inverted at this step.
1378	    \item \textbf{Reindex the torsion cokernel.} Apply \cref{lem:gf_torsion_reindex} to the
1379	      finite torsion module \(T\): there are \(g \ne 0\) in \(A\) and \(m' < d\) such that
1380	      the localisation \(T_g\) is module-finite over the polynomial ring
1381	      \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\) in fewer variables over the base ring \(A_g = A[g^{-1}]\).
1382	    \item \textbf{Inductive hypothesis at the new base \(A_g\).} Since \(A_g\) is again a
1383	      noetherian domain and \(m' < d\), the inductive hypothesis applies \emph{at base
1384	      \(A_g\)} to the finite \(A_g[X_1, \dots, X_{m'}]\)-module \(T_g\): there is
1385	      \(h \ne 0\) in \(A_g\) with \((T_g)_h\) free over \((A_g)_h\). This is exactly the
1386	      point at which the base ring of the induction must be allowed to change from \(A\)
1387	      to \(A_g\).
1388	    \item \textbf{Descend the witness from \(A_g\) to \(A\).} Clearing the denominator of
1389	      \(h\) over \(A_g\) produces a numerator \(a \in A\) whose image in \(A_g\) is associated
1390	      to \(h\); then the iterated localisation \((A_g)_h\) is the single localisation
1391	      \(A_{g a}\) and \((T_g)_h\) is \(T_{g a}\). Thus freeness of \((T_g)_h\) over
1392	      \((A_g)_h\) transports to freeness of \(T_{g a}\) over \(A_{g a}\) by the
1393	      iterated-\(\mathrm{Away}\)-tower descent \cref{lem:gf_away_tower_descent}. Set
1394	      \(f := g a \ne 0\) — the single product, \emph{not} a square — so that \(T_f\) is free
1395	      over \(A_f\).
1396	      % NOTE: the tower-descent helper lem:gf_away_tower_descent
1397	      % (free_localizationAway_of_away_tower) is now CLOSED and axiom-clean. The L5 sorry that
1398	      % remains is NOT a missing math step — it is a Lean elaboration blocker: the
1399	      % `OreLocalization` instance *presentations* on the localised quotient `(N ⧸ range φ)_g`
1400	      % (CommSemiring / AddCommMonoid / Module-SMul) emitted by `gf_torsion_reindex` are defeq
1401	      % but not instance-transparent-equal to the presentations the helper input expects. The
1402	      % clean fix is non-local: make `gf_torsion_reindex` emit its conclusion over the canonical
1403	      % `OreLocalization.*` instances (its final `exact` / existential binders), after which the
1404	      % recorded five-line assembly closes verbatim. Do NOT re-dispatch a raw L5 prover round
1405	      % without that instance-alignment refactor first.
1406	    \item \textbf{Splice.} The left end \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) of the sequence is a free
1407	      \(A\)-module (the polynomial ring \(P_d = A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) is \(A\)-free, hence
1408	      so is any finite direct power; note \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) is \emph{not} module-finite
1409	      over \(A\), but freeness is all the splice requires), and a free module stays free
1410	      under the localisation \(A \to A_f\); so \(P_d^{\oplus m}\) localises to a free
1411	      \(A_f\)-module. With both ends free over \(A_f\) after inverting \(f = g a\),
1412	      \cref{lem:gf_splice_shortExact} splices the localised short exact sequence to
1413	      conclude that the middle term \(N_f\) is free over \(A_f\). Taking \(f = g a\)
1414	      completes the induction.
1415	  \end{enumerate}
1416	\end{proof}

1418	\subsection{Supporting finite-module helpers}
1419	\label{sec:gf_finite_helpers}
1420	
1421	The following two lemmas package the finite \(A\)-module leaf in the two forms
1422	(flatness and module-finiteness) used by the dévissage chain.
1423	
1424	\begin{lemma}
1425	
1426	
1427	[Flat localization away, finite module]
1428	  \label{lem:gf_flat_finite}
1429	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite}
1430	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1431	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain and \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module. Then there exists
1432	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\) is flat over \(A_f\).
1433	\end{lemma}
1434	\begin{proof}
1435	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1436	  By \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} \(M_f\) is free over \(A_f\); free modules are flat.
1437	\end{proof}
1438	
1439	\begin{lemma}
1440	
1441	
1442	[Free localization away, module-finite variant]
1443	  \label{lem:gf_free_moduleFinite}
1444	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GenericFreeness.exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite}
1445	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1446	  Let \(A\) be a noetherian domain, \(B\) a module-finite \(A\)-algebra, and \(M\) a
1447	  finite \(B\)-module with the compatible \(A\)-module structure via the scalar tower
1448	  \(A \to B \to M\). Then there exists \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that \(M_f\)
1449	  is free over \(A_f\).
1450	\end{lemma}
1451	\begin{proof}
1452	  \uses{lem:gf_finite_module}
1453	  Since \(B\) is module-finite over \(A\) and \(M\) is module-finite over \(B\),
1454	  transitivity of module-finiteness makes \(M\) a finite \(A\)-module.
1455	  \cref{lem:gf_finite_module} then gives the required \(f\).
1456	\end{proof}

1458	\subsection{Geometric bridges for the affine assembly}
1459	\label{sec:gf_geometric_bridges}
1460	
1461	The geometric form of generic flatness is assembled from the algebraic core by
1462	restricting to a non-empty affine open of \(S\) and working patchwise over a finite
1463	affine cover of the preimage. Two genuinely geometric steps are isolated here as
1464	named bridges: the affine-local finiteness of the section module of a finite-type
1465	quasi-coherent sheaf (G1), feeding the algebraic core its finite-module input; and
1466	the flat-locality assembly (G3), promoting per-patch freeness to flatness of the
1467	restricted sheaf over the base open. Neither is supplied directly by Mathlib: Mathlib
1468	does not provide the affine-local identification of \(\F|_W\) with the associated sheaf
1469	of \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) together with the finiteness this transports.
1470	
1471	\begin{lemma}[G1 — finite-type quasi-coherent sheaf has finite affine sections]
1472	  \label{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1473	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1474	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1475	  % LEAN STATUS: G1 — project-built (mathlib-build target this iter); Mathlib has only
1476	  % the abstract SheafOfModules.IsFiniteType / IsQuasicoherent local-generator
1477	  % predicates, not this affine-local finiteness.
1478	  % NOTE: overlaps the QUOT keystone lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen — to be
1479	  % extracted to shared qcoh-affine-local infra (see STRATEGY Open questions).
1480	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB
1481	  % (Lemma "Characterizing modules of finite type", \S "Characterizing modules of
1482	  % finite type and finite presentation")
1483	  % (read from references/stacks-properties.tex, L2092-L2098).
1484	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1485	  % "Let $X = \Spec(R)$ be an affine scheme.
1486	  %  The quasi-coherent sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules
1487	  %  $\widetilde M$ is a finite type $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
1488	  %  if and only if $M$ is a finite $R$-module."
1489	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1490	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\F : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) a quasi-coherent sheaf of
1491	  modules with \(\F\) of finite type. Then for every affine open \(W \subseteq X\), the
1492	  section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite module over \(\Gamma(X, W)\).
1493	\end{lemma}
1494	
1495	\begin{proof}
1496	  \uses{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}
1497	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the comment above the statement (Tag 01PB, L2092-L2098;
1498	  % the Stacks proof reduces finite-type on a standard open cover to the algebra
1499	  % gluing lemma for finite generation of $M$ from finite generation of each $M_{f_i}$).
1500	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", Tag 01PB.}
1501	  Fix an affine open \(W \subseteq X\) and write \(R = \Gamma(X, W)\) and
1502	  \(N = \Gamma(\F, W)\). Identifying \(W\) with \(\Spec R\), quasi-coherence of \(\F\)
1503	  presents \(\F|_W\) as the associated sheaf \(\widetilde N\) of the \(R\)-module \(N\):
1504	  on a basic open \(D(g) \subseteq W\) the sections are the localized module
1505	  \(\Gamma(\F, D(g)) = N_g\) over \(\Gamma(X, D(g)) = R_g\)
1506	  (\cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}). The finite-type hypothesis on
1507	  \(\F\) says \(\F\) is locally generated by finitely many sections; restricted to the
1508	  quasi-compact affine \(W\), this yields a standard open cover
1509	  \(W = \bigcup_{i=1}^{n} D(g_i)\) on each member of which \(\widetilde N|_{D(g_i)}\) is
1510	  generated by finitely many sections, i.e.\ each localization \(N_{g_i}\) is a finite
1511	  \(R_{g_i}\)-module. The elements \(g_1, \dots, g_n\) generate the unit ideal of \(R\)
1512	  (the \(D(g_i)\) cover \(\Spec R\)); by the standard finite-generation gluing
1513	  criterion — finite generation of \(N\) over \(R\) is local on such a cover — the
1514	  module \(N\) is finite over \(R\). Hence \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is a finite
1515	  \(\Gamma(X, W)\)-module.
1516	\end{proof}
1517	
1518	\begin{lemma}[G3 — flat-locality assembly from per-patch freeness]
1519	  \label{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1520	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1521	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1522	  % LEAN STATUS: G3 — project-built; the geometric glue identifying Γ(F, W∩W_j)
1523	  % localisations across the source cover and the base-restriction is the missing
1524	  % plumbing. Stub-level this iter; built after G1.
1525	  Let \(p : X \to S\) be a finite-type morphism with \(S\) integral and locally
1526	  noetherian, and \(\F\) a finite-type quasi-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Fix a non-empty
1527	  affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\) with \(A\) a noetherian domain, a finite
1528	  affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_j\) of \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), and an element
1529	  \(f \in A\), \(f \ne 0\), such that for every \(j\) the localized section module
1530	  \((M_j)_f\) (with \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\)) is free over \(A_f\). Set
1531	  \(V = D(f) \subseteq U_0\). Then for every affine open \(U \le V\) of \(S\) and every
1532	  affine open \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\) of \(X\), the section module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat
1533	  over \(\Gamma(S, U)\).
1534	\end{lemma}
1535	
1536	\begin{proof}
1537	  \uses{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1538	  Free modules are flat (\(\OO\)-flatness of a free module), so each \((M_j)_f\) is flat
1539	  over \(A_f\). Flatness of a module is local on the source: a module is flat over a
1540	  ring iff it is flat after localizing at every maximal ideal, and the family
1541	  \(\{W_j\}\) covers \(p^{-1}(U_0)\), so the per-patch flatness statements assemble to
1542	  flatness of the section module of \(\F\) over \(A_f = \Gamma(S, V)\) on the whole
1543	  preimage \(p^{-1}(V)\). The flat locus is moreover stable under base-restriction along
1544	  the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow V\): for an affine \(U \le V\) the ring
1545	  \(\Gamma(S, U)\) is a localization of \(\Gamma(S, V) = A_f\) (an open immersion of
1546	  affines into a basic open being a localization), and for an affine \(W \le p^{-1}(U)\)
1547	  the module \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is the corresponding localization of the section module
1548	  over the relevant patch \(W_j\) (via \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections}
1549	  identifying the finite section modules and their localizations across the cover).
1550	  Flatness is preserved under localization of both the ring and the module, so
1551	  \(\Gamma(\F, W)\) is flat over \(\Gamma(S, U)\), as claimed.
1552	\end{proof}

1582	  %       quantified over affine opens U ≤ V of S and affine opens W ≤ p⁻¹U of X, with
1583	  %       Γ(F,W) given its Γ(S,U)-module structure via p.appLE — verbatim form in the
1584	  %       .lean file, the source of truth).

1587	  % entail QuasiCompact; without it the statement is false (counterexample:
1588	  % ⊔_i Spec ℤ → Spec ℤ with a finite-type torsion sheaf). It is the extra clause
1589	  % promoting `LocallyOfFiniteType` to `FiniteType`, and there is no instance
1590	  % `LocallyOfFiniteType → QuasiCompact`. The header above carries it, and the Step-1
1591	  % proof prose below invokes it directly (no false "finite type ⟹ quasi-compact" step).

1593	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''
1594	  % (read from references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1781-L1787).
1595	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
1596	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian and integral scheme.
1597	  %  Let \(p: X\to S\) be a finite type morphism, and let \(\F\) be a
1598	  %  coherent sheaf of \({\mathcal O}_X\)-modules. Then there exists a non-empty
1599	  %  open subscheme \(U\subset S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\)
1600	  %  to \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is flat over \({\mathcal O}_U\)."
1601	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S4, Theorem ``generic flatness''.}
1602	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian integral scheme, \(p : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
1603	  and \(\F\) a coherent \(\OO_X\)-module. Then there exists a non-empty open
1604	  subscheme \(U \subseteq S\) such that the restriction of \(\F\) to
1605	  \(X_U = p^{-1}(U)\) is \(\OO_U\)-flat.
1606	
1607	  The coherence hypothesis on \(\F\) is essential: a non-coherent \(\OO_X\)-module
1608	  need not become flat over any non-empty open of \(S\).
1609	\end{theorem}
1610	
1611	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The above theorem has the following consequence, which
1612	% follows by restricting attention to a non-empty affine open subscheme of \(S\)."
1613	% (Source: nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L1776-L1777. The
1614	% Nitsure proof is the single sentence quoted: deduce the geometric form from
1615	% the algebraic generic flatness statement (\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic})
1616	% by restricting to a non-empty
1617	% affine open of \(S\), where the situation reduces to the algebraic statement.)
1618	\begin{proof}
1619	  \uses{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic, lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections,
1620	        lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly}
1621	  Restrict to a non-empty affine open \(U_0 = \Spec A \subseteq S\), where \(A\) is a
1622	  noetherian domain (\(S\) integral and locally noetherian). We assemble the witness
1623	  \(V\) from the algebraic form on a finite affine cover.
1624	
1625	  \emph{Step 1 — finite affine cover above \(U_0\).} Since \(p\) is quasi-compact (by
1626	  the \([\mathrm{QuasiCompact}\,p]\) hypothesis) and \(U_0\) is affine hence
1627	  quasi-compact, the preimage \(X_{U_0} = p^{-1}(U_0)\) is quasi-compact; choose a
1628	  finite affine cover \(\{W_j = \Spec B_j\}_{j}\) of \(X_{U_0}\) by affine opens of
1629	  \(X\). Because \(p\) is locally of finite type, each ring \(B_j = \Gamma(X, W_j)\)
1630	  is a finite-type \(A\)-algebra.
1631	
1632	  \emph{Step 2 — read off a finite module on each patch.} On each \(W_j\), quasi-coherence
1633	  of \(\F\) identifies \(\F|_{W_j}\) with the associated sheaf \(\widetilde{M_j}\) of the
1634	  section module \(M_j = \Gamma(\F, W_j)\), and the finite-section bridge
1635	  \cref{lem:gf_qcoh_fintype_finite_sections} — applied to the affine open \(W_j\) — makes
1636	  \(M_j\) a finite \(B_j\)-module. Through the scalar tower \(A \to B_j \to M_j\),
1637	  \(M_j\) is the data fed to the algebraic form.
1638	
1639	  \emph{Step 3 — apply the algebraic form and take the common basic open.}
1640	  \cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic} applied to \((A, B_j, M_j)\) yields \(f_j \in A\),
1641	  \(f_j \ne 0\), with \((M_j)_{f_j}\) free over \(A_{f_j}\). As the cover is finite, set
1642	  \(f := \prod_j f_j \ne 0\) (a non-zero element of the domain \(A\)) and
1643	  \(V := D(f) \subseteq \Spec A \subseteq S\), a non-empty basic open; over \(V\) every
1644	  \((M_j)_f\) is free over \(A_f\).
1645	
1646	  \emph{Step 4 — freeness implies flatness.} On each patch the localized section module
1647	  is free, hence flat over \(A_f\); the flat-locality assembly
1648	  \cref{lem:gf_flat_locality_assembly} promotes these per-patch freeness statements to
1649	  the fibrewise flatness conclusion, via the criterion that a module flat at every
1650	  maximal ideal is flat together with the locality of flatness on the source cover. This
1651	  produces the required \(V\) with \(\F|_{X_V}\) flat over \(\OO_V\).
1652	\end{proof}

1654	\section{Mathlib status}
1655	\label{sec:flatstrat_mathlib_status}
1656	
1657	Mathlib (master \texttt{b80f227}) provides \texttt{Module.Flat}
1658	(\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Basic}), the module-theoretic notion of
1659	flatness applicable affine-locally to the section modules of \(\F\), together
1660	with the dévissage and Noether-normalisation infrastructure that the algebraic
1661	generic-flatness argument consumes
1662	(\texttt{IsNoetherianRing.induction\_on\_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime},
1663	\texttt{Module.FinitePresentation.exists\_free\_localizedModule\_powers},
1664	\texttt{exists\_finite\_inj\_algHom\_of\_fg}).  It does \emph{not} yet contain
1665	the polynomial-ring core of generic freeness (a finite module over
1666	\(A[X_1, \dots, X_d]\) becomes free after inverting a single element), nor the
1667	geometric statement \texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness}
1668	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness}) packaging the algebraic form
1669	(\cref{thm:generic_flatness_algebraic}) over a noetherian integral base.  These
1670	are introduced in
1671	\texttt{AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/FlatteningStratification.lean}.

2823	% opens of S') and lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange (the section-

2825	% dictionaries -- lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso and lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso --
2826	% feed the second obligation. cancelBaseChange is in Mathlib with no flatness; the
2827	% affine case carries no flatness hypothesis. No Čech infrastructure is used here.
2828	\begin{proof}
2829	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso, lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso, lem:base_change_map_affine_local, lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}
2830	  The proof is the composite of the two named obligations, taken \emph{directly on
2831	  global sections}.
2832	
2833	  \medskip
2834	  \emph{First reduction (locality on \(S'\)).} By
2835	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} it suffices to prove that the
2836	  base-change map of Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an
2837	  isomorphism on sections over every affine open of \(S'\). This is exactly the
2838	  locality reduction Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_map_affine_local}: restricting the
2839	  cartesian square to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R') \subseteq S'\) and
2840	  a chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), the
2841	  hypothesis \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to
2842	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and quasi-coherence of \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict
2843	  to \(\widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\). (It is here that quasi-coherence is
2844	  used: it is what licenses writing \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\).) Computing the
2845	  fibre product gives \(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\) and
2846	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) the tilde of the \((R' \otimes_R A)\)-module
2847	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\). The matter is thus reduced to the affine--affine
2848	  model, where the base-change map is a morphism \(\alpha\) of quasi-coherent
2849	  \((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules.
2850	
2851	  \medskip
2852	  \emph{Passage to global sections.} The functor
2853	  \(\widetilde{(-)} : \operatorname{ModuleCat}(R') \to
2854	  (\operatorname{Spec} R').\mathrm{Modules}\) is fully faithful with essential image
2855	  the quasi-coherent modules; equivalently the counit
2856	  \(\widetilde{\Gamma(\mathcal{N})} \to \mathcal{N}\) is an isomorphism exactly when
2857	  \(\mathcal{N}\) is quasi-coherent. Source and target of \(\alpha\) are
2858	  quasi-coherent, so both counits are isomorphisms and, by their naturality,
2859	  \[
2860	    \mathrm{IsIso}\,\alpha
2861	    \;\Longleftrightarrow\;
2862	    \mathrm{IsIso}\bigl(\Gamma(\alpha)\bigr),
2863	  \]
2864	  where \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is a concrete \(R'\)-linear map in
2865	  \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\). The whole computation therefore stays at the level
2866	  of modules.
2867	
2868	  \medskip
2869	  \emph{Section-level identification (the second obligation).} By the section-level
2870	  computation Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, the map
2871	  \(\Gamma(\alpha)\), with its domain \(\Gamma(g^*(f_*\widetilde{M}))\) identified with
2872	  \(R' \otimes_R M\) and its codomain \(\Gamma(f'_*(g')^*\widetilde{M})\) identified
2873	  with \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\) through the two dictionaries
2874	  Lemmas~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso},
2875	  \emph{is} the canonical cancellation isomorphism
2876	  \[
2877	    \operatorname{cancelBaseChange} :
2878	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M,
2879	    \qquad (r' \otimes a) \otimes m \;\mapsto\; r' \otimes (a \cdot m)
2880	  \]
2881	  of \(R'\)-modules (Lemma~\ref{lem:cancelBaseChange_mathlib}). This is the entire
2882	  content of the affine close, and it is performed \emph{directly on the module of
2883	  global sections} — there is no separate abstract adjoint-mate identification at the
2884	  level of sheaves. Because \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) is an isomorphism with
2885	  no flatness hypothesis whatsoever, \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) is an isomorphism, hence so is
2886	  \(\alpha\) on the affine chart, and by the locality reduction so is the global
2887	  base-change map. Flatness of \(g\) plays no role in the affine lemma; it enters only
2888	  in the global statement Theorem~\ref{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}, where it is
2889	  used to commute \(- \otimes_A B\) with the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\).
2890	\end{proof}

2892	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
2893	
2894	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
2895	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
2896	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
2897	
2898	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
2899	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2900	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
2901	  \mathlibok
2902	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
2903	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
2904	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
2905	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
2906	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
2907	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
2908	  \[
2909	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
2910	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
2911	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
2912	  \]
2913	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
2914	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
2915	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
2916	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
2917	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
2918	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
2919	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
2920	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
2921	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
2922	\end{lemma}
2923	
2924	\begin{theorem}\leanok
2925	[Flat base change, $i=0$ case]
2926	  \label{thm:flat_base_change_pushforward}
2927	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso}
2928	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward,
2929	        lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
2930	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Tag 02KH (Lemma "Flat base change", part for
2931	  % $i = 0$) (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L947--970, and
2932	  % references/stacks-coherent.md).
2933	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Consider a cartesian diagram of schemes
2934	  % $$
2935	  % \xymatrix{
2936	  % X' \ar[d]_{f'} \ar[r]_{g'} & X \ar[d]^f \\
2937	  % S' \ar[r]^g & S
2938	  % }
2939	  % $$
2940	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent $\mathcal{O}_X$-module
2941	  % with pullback $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$.
2942	  % Assume that $g$ is flat and that $f$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated.
2943	  % For any $i \geq 0$
2944	  % \begin{enumerate}
2945	  % \item the base change map of
2946	  % Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-base-change-map-flat-case}
2947	  % is an isomorphism
2948	  % $$
2949	  % g^*R^if_*\mathcal{F} \longrightarrow R^if'_*\mathcal{F}',
2950	  % $$
2951	  % \item if $S = \Spec(A)$ and $S' = \Spec(B)$, then
2952	  % $H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B = H^i(X', \mathcal{F}')$.
2953	  % \end{enumerate}"
2954	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Tag 02KH (``Flat base change''), the
2955	  \(i = 0\) case.}
2956	  Consider the cartesian square
2957	  \[
2958	    \begin{array}{ccc}
2959	      X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
2960	      {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
2961	      S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
2962	    \end{array}
2963	  \]
2964	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
2965	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
2966	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
2967	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
2968	  \[
2969	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2970	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
2971	  \]
2972	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
2973	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
2974	  \[
2975	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
2976	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
2977	  \]
2978	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
2979	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
2980	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
2981	
2982	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
2983	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
2984	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
2985	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
2986	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
2987	  an isomorphism.
2988	\end{theorem}

3031	% $$

3033	% $$
3034	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}_B, \mathcal{F}_B) =
3035	% \check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathcal{U}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
3036	% $$

3038	% Lemma \ref{lemma-affine-base-change}.
3039	% Since $A \to B$ is flat, the same thing remains true on taking cohomology.
3040	%

3042	% $\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \ldots \cup U_t$. We will use the
3043	% {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral sequence
3044	% Cohomology, Lemma \ref{cohomology-lemma-cech-spectral-sequence}.

3048	% NOTE: For the $i = 0$ case the argument is Čech-free: $H^0(X,\mathcal{F})$ is the
3049	% sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover (Čech degree 0/1, NOT Čech
3050	% cohomology -- no Leray, no spectral sequence), each term base-changes by the affine
3051	% lemma lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, and flatness of $A \to B$ commutes
3052	% $-\otimes_A B$ with that finite equalizer via lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib
3053	% (Mathlib's LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv). The quasi-separated case is the
3054	% Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number of cover members named in the source quote.
3055	\begin{proof}
3056	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:affine_base_change_pushforward, lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3057	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
3058	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
3059	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
3060	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
3061	  sequence.
3062	
3063	  \medskip
3064	  \emph{Reduction to the affine target.} The statement is local on \(S'\), so we
3065	  may assume \(S\) and \(S'\) are affine, say \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
3066	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat. Because \(f\) is
3067	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated, \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent on
3068	  \(S\); it is therefore the sheaf associated to the \(A\)-module
3069	  \(H^0(S, f_*\mathcal{F}) = H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})\). Likewise
3070	  \(f'_*\mathcal{F}'\) is the sheaf on \(S'\) associated to the \(B\)-module
3071	  \(H^0(X', \mathcal{F}') = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). Since pullback along \(g\)
3072	  corresponds to \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules, the base-change map becomes the
3073	  comparison map of \(B\)-modules
3074	  \[
3075	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
3076	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
3077	  \]
3078	  and it suffices to prove that this map is an isomorphism.
3079	
3080	  \medskip
3081	  \emph{\(H^0\) as a finite equalizer (the sheaf condition).} Choose a finite affine
3082	  open cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\) (possible since \(X\) is
3083	  quasi-compact). The sheaf condition for \(\mathcal{F}\) on this cover presents the
3084	  global sections as the equalizer of the two restriction maps
3085	  \[
3086	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
3087	    \;=\;
3088	    \operatorname{eq}\Bigl(
3089	      \textstyle\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F})
3090	      \;\rightrightarrows\;
3091	      \textstyle\prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})
3092	    \Bigr),
3093	    \qquad U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j,
3094	  \]
3095	  where the two parallel maps are \(s \mapsto (s_i|_{U_{ij}})\) and
3096	  \(s \mapsto (s_j|_{U_{ij}})\). This is precisely Čech degree \(0\) into degree
3097	  \(1\); it is the sheaf axiom and requires \emph{no} separatedness of \(X\) and no
3098	  higher cohomology.
3099	
3100	  \medskip
3101	  \emph{The separated case.} Suppose first that \(X\) is separated over \(A\). Then
3102	  every intersection \(U_{ij}\) is affine. Base changing the cover yields a finite
3103	  affine cover \(\mathcal{U}_B : X_B = (U_1)_B \cup \dots \cup (U_t)_B\) of \(X_B\),
3104	  and the same sheaf-condition equalizer presents \(\Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\). The
3105	  affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward} (in its
3106	  \(i = 0\)/global-sections form) identifies each base-changed term with the original
3107	  term tensored over \(A\) with \(B\):
3108	  \[
3109	    \Gamma\bigl((U_i)_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3110	    \qquad
3111	    \Gamma\bigl((U_{ij})_B, \mathcal{F}_B\bigr) \cong \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B,
3112	  \]
3113	  compatibly with the two restriction maps. Hence the equalizer diagram for \(X_B\) is
3114	  the diagram for \(X\) with \(- \otimes_A B\) applied (using that \(- \otimes_A B\)
3115	  commutes with the finite products \(\prod_i\), \(\prod_{i,j}\)). Because \(A \to B\)
3116	  is flat, base change commutes with the formation of this finite equalizer
3117	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}):
3118	  \[
3119	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B
3120	    = \operatorname{eq}(\cdots) \otimes_A B
3121	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3122	    \operatorname{eq}(\cdots \otimes_A B)
3123	    = \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
3124	  \]
3125	  This is exactly the comparison map of the reduction step, so it is an isomorphism.
3126	
3127	  \medskip
3128	  \emph{The quasi-separated case.} In general \(X\) need only be quasi-separated. We
3129	  argue by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the number \(t\) of members of a finite affine
3130	  cover \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\), each step reducing to the
3131	  separated/affine case. For \(t = 1\), \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the
3132	  affine lemma. For \(t > 1\), set \(V = U_t\) (affine) and
3133	  \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (an open covered by \(t - 1\) affines). Their
3134	  intersection \(W \cap V = \bigcup_{i < t}(U_i \cap U_t)\) is covered by the
3135	  \(t - 1\) opens \(U_i \cap U_t\), each of which is quasi-compact and separated
3136	  (an open of the affine, hence separated, \(U_i\)); thus \(W \cap V\) is separated
3137	  and quasi-compact, and flat base change holds for it by the separated case. The
3138	  two-member cover \(\{W, V\}\) of \(X\) gives the left-exact Mayer--Vietoris
3139	  sequence — itself the sheaf-condition equalizer for that cover —
3140	  \[
3141	    0 \longrightarrow \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})
3142	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W, \mathcal{F}) \oplus \Gamma(V, \mathcal{F})
3143	      \longrightarrow \Gamma(W \cap V, \mathcal{F}),
3144	  \]
3145	  and likewise for \(X_B\) with \(\{W_B, V_B\}\). Flatness of \(A \to B\) makes
3146	  \(- \otimes_A B\) preserve this finite kernel/equalizer
3147	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}); since flat base change holds for
3148	  \(V\) (affine), for \(W\) (inductive hypothesis, \(t - 1\) members), and for
3149	  \(W \cap V\) (separated), it follows for \(X\). In degree \(0\) this gives
3150	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \cong \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)\), as required.
3151	\end{proof}

1858	  \(P_1 \subseteq P_2\) gives a surjection \(N/P_1 \twoheadrightarrow N/P_2\)
1859	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}), and finiteness transfers along it
1860	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}). This supplies the

1863	\end{lemma}

1865	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1866	
1867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1868	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1869	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1871	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1872	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1873	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1874	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1875	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1876	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1877	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1878	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1879	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1880	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1881	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1882	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1883	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).
1884	
1885	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1886	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1887	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1888	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1889	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1890	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1891	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.
1892	\end{lemma}
1893	
1894	\begin{proof}
1895	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1896	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1897	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1898	    \leanok
1899	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1900	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1901	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1902	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1903	  \[
1904	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1905	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1906	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1907	  \]
1908	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1909	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1910	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1911	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1912	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1913	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1914	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1915	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1916	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1917	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1918	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1919	  \[
1920	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1921	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1922	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1923	  \]
1924	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1925	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1926	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1927	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1928	
1929	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1930	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1931	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1932	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1933	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1934	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1935	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1936	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1937	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1938	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1939	\end{proof}

1941	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1942	
1943	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1944	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1945	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1946	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1947	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1948	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1949	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.
1950	
1951	\begin{lemma}
1952	
1953	\leanok
1954	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1955	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1956	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1957	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1958	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1959	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1960	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1961	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1962	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1963	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)
1964	  (\cref{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}). This full stability is what allows the
1965	  kernel subquotient's finiteness to be measured over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1966	  before the transfer drops the last variable.
1967	\end{lemma}
1968	
1969	\begin{lemma}
1970	
1971	\leanok
1972	[The cokernel pair's upper module is stable under the full family]
1973	  \label{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}
1974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_stable_full}
1975	  \uses{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1976	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1977	  stable under every \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)): each \(t_i\) preserves \(N'\) and
1978	  preserves the image \(x \cdot N\) because it commutes with \(x\) and preserves
1979	  \(N\) (\cref{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}), and a map preserves a sum of
1980	  preserved submodules. This full stability supports the over-\(\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1981	  t_r]\) finiteness input of the cokernel constructor.
1982	\end{lemma}
1983	
1984	\begin{definition}
1985	
1986	\leanok
1987	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1988	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}
1989	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1990	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1991	    lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1992	    lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_ker_stable_full,
1993	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1994	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1995	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1996	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1997	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1998	  \[
1999	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
2000	  \]
2001	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
2002	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
2003	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
2004	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
2005	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
2006	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to
2007	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}) to get
2008	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
2009	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
2010	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
2011	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
2012	  length-\(r\) datum.
2013	\end{definition}
2014	
2015	\begin{definition}
2016	
2017	\leanok
2018	[Cokernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
2019	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}
2020	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.coker}
2021	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2022	    lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_coker_annihilate,
2023	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_coker_stable_full,
2024	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
2025	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
2026	  From the same length-\((r+1)\) datum, the \emph{cokernel constructor} produces the
2027	  length-\(r\) datum
2028	  \[
2029	    \operatorname{coker} D \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr)
2030	  \]
2031	  on the reduced family. Its non-finiteness fields come from the ambient cokernel
2032	  calculus: homogeneity of \(N' + x \cdot N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}),
2033	  the nesting \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_le}), and stability
2034	  under the reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}). The finiteness field
2035	  is obtained by enlarging the denominator from \(N'\) to \(N' + x \cdot N\)
2036	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}) to get
2037	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the cokernel subquotient, then
2038	  transferring down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}),
2039	  valid because \(x\) annihilates the cokernel subquotient
2040	  (\cref{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}). This realises the cokernel half of
2041	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine length-\(r\) datum.
2042	\end{definition}

2044	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2045	
2046	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2047	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2048	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2051	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2052	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2053	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2054	  \[
2055	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2056	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2057	  \]
2058	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2060	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2061	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2062	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2063	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2065	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2066	  \[
2067	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2068	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2071	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2072	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2073	\end{lemma}
2074	
2075	\begin{proof}
2076	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2077	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2078	    \leanok
2079	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2080	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2081	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2083	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2084	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2085	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2086	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2088	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2089	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2090	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2091	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2092	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2093	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2094	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2095	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2096	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2097	\end{proof}
